title: LLM Output Content Policy Violation Logging
app: Azure OpenAI content filter logs, OpenAI moderation API results
spl:
index=ai_ops (sourcetype="azure:openai" OR sourcetype="openai:api")
| search content_filter_result="blocked" OR finish_reason="content_filter" OR moderation_flagged="true"
| stats count by filter_category, model, app_id
| sort -count
description: Provider and organizational safety filters block harmful content; logging violations supports red-teaming, policy tuning, and audit trails for regulated use cases.
value: Provider and organizational safety filters block harmful content; logging violations supports red-teaming, policy tuning, and audit trails for regulated use cases.
implementation: Capture moderation and content-filter outcomes from API responses (categories, severity). Avoid storing blocked text; store hashes or length only if needed. Review spikes by app or model after prompt changes. Feed executive summary dashboards for AI governance.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Azure OpenAI content filter logs, OpenAI moderation API results.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=azure:openai`, `sourcetype=openai:api`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Capture moderation and content-filter outcomes from API responses (categories, severity). Avoid storing blocked text; store hashes or length only if needed. Review spikes by app or model after prompt changes. Feed executive summary dashboards for AI governance.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ai_ops (sourcetype="azure:openai" OR sourcetype="openai:api")
| search content_filter_result="blocked" OR finish_reason="content_filter" OR moderation_flagged="true"
| stats count by filter_category, model, app_id
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**LLM Output Content Policy Violation Logging** — Provider and organizational safety filters block harmful content; logging violations supports red-teaming, policy tuning, and audit trails for regulated use cases.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=azure:openai`, `sourcetype=openai:api`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Azure OpenAI content filter logs, OpenAI moderation API results. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ai_ops; **sourcetype**: azure:openai, openai:api. Those sourcetypes align with what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=ai_ops, sourcetype="azure:openai". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by filter_category, model, app_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (violations by category), Line chart (trend), Table (top apps).
visualization: Bar chart (violations by category), Line chart (trend), Table (top apps).
